Q1: My study routine was honestly quite chaotic, but I made it work with what I had. I usually studied late at night after my part-time job and other responsibilities were done. I'd block out chunks of time on weekends when I could, maybe four or five hours at a stretch. I kept a notebook where I'd write down everything important from the readings since I learn best through writing things out. It wasn't perfect, but I tried to be consistent even when life got in the way.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be on top of things with regular evening sessions, but then something would come up—extra shifts at work or family obligations—and I'd have to cram everything closer to deadlines. I tried to study at least a bit every day, even if it was just reviewing my handwritten notes on the bus. The inconsistency stressed me out, but I learned to adapt.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new assignments or announcements that got me to log in. I'd also log in when I knew discussion posts were due because participating in those was part of my grade. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about something and that would prompt me to check. Honestly, anxiety about missing something important was often what pushed me to log in, even when I was exhausted.
Q4: There was a period mid-semester when logging in became more automatic for me. I started checking every morning before work, just a quick look at announcements and any new materials. The discussions actually helped with this because I knew other students would be posting and I wanted to stay part of the conversation. It felt less like a chore once I connected with a few peers who were also juggling a lot.
Q5: The reading materials and lecture notes were most useful because I could download them and study offline. I'd print things out when I could afford to, or copy key passages into my notebook. The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful too—seeing how other students interpreted the material gave me different perspectives. I also appreciated when instructors posted clear summaries because it helped me focus on what really mattered.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skipped the videos or only watched parts of them. They took too long and my internet at home isn't always reliable, so streaming was frustrating. I'd try to find the same information in the written materials instead. Some of the supplementary readings I ignored because I simply didn't have time—I had to prioritize what was graded.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. If I knew something would help me pass, I was motivated to engage with it despite being tired. I also valued activities that I could complete in manageable chunks since I rarely had long uninterrupted periods. Anything that helped me understand the core concepts without too much fluff felt worthwhile.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. When I was managing my time well, I'd try to do some of them because I genuinely wanted to learn more. But most of the time, required work took priority because I couldn't risk my grade slipping. There was always this guilt about not doing the optional stuff, like I was missing out on deeper learning.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then study from printed copies or my notebook during my commute or breaks at work. I also used a few free resources I found online—YouTube explanations, articles—that helped clarify concepts. My notebook was basically my second brain; I'd rewrite and reorganize information constantly.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with the material but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd spend hours rewriting my notes by hand to memorize concepts, or when I'd discuss topics with a classmate over the phone. I'd also review printed materials multiple times before exams. All that effort was invisible online but it was real learning happening.
Q11: Yes, this was my main strategy actually. I'd access materials once, download everything I could, and then work from those files for days or weeks. My internet wasn't always reliable, so having offline copies was essential. I'd annotate PDFs on my phone or print them when possible. The LMS was more like a library I'd visit to collect resources rather than a place I'd stay.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. I was working extra hours to cover some unexpected expenses, and I was exhausted all the time. My attendance dropped because I physically couldn't make it to some classes. I felt overwhelmed and started doubting whether I could finish the course. The mindfulness exercises helped a bit with the anxiety, but the stress was still very real.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued from work, I'd log into the LMS but barely absorb anything—just going through the motions. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and earning money, and money usually won. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections until I absolutely had to face them for an assignment.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have videos playing but my mind was elsewhere. I'd be thinking about bills or family stuff while technically being 'active' on the platform. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph multiple times without it sinking in. Those sessions felt unproductive even though the system probably recorded them as engagement.
Q15: I postponed difficult assignments more than I should have. When something felt overwhelming or I didn't understand it, I'd put it off hoping I'd feel more capable later. Fear of doing poorly made me avoid starting sometimes. I knew it was counterproductive, but the anxiety made it hard to just dive in. I'd eventually push through, but often last minute.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. I'd structure my whole week around what was due and when. The closer a deadline got, the more intensely I'd engage with the LMS and related materials. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted because there was always something else demanding my attention. They created pressure but also necessary structure.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I got encouraging comments, I felt motivated to try harder on the next assignment. Critical feedback sometimes made me anxious, but I tried to use it constructively. I wished feedback came faster though—sometimes by the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. Quick feedback would have helped me adjust sooner.
Q18: The course structure was okay but not ideal for someone with my schedule. Weekly modules helped me know what to expect, but some weeks were heavier than others and that unpredictability was stressful. I appreciated when things were clearly laid out in advance so I could plan around my work shifts. More flexibility in deadlines would have helped me a lot.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement much easier for me. The discussion forums were also good because I could participate asynchronously. What made it harder was when live sessions were scheduled at times I couldn't attend, or when materials were only available through streaming. Clear organization helped; when things were scattered, I'd waste time just finding what I needed.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is searching for understanding but struggling to find it. They might watch videos repeatedly or revisit materials because they're confused, not because they're procrastinating. Or maybe life circumstances prevent them from completing work even though they're trying to learn. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's a sign of struggle.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I didn't have super high LMS activity, but I was studying a lot offline and still managed to pass. If you only looked at my login frequency, you'd think I wasn't trying, but I was putting in twenty hours a week through other means. Some of us just learn better with physical materials or have constraints that keep us offline.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in, what they click on, and how long they stay. That's useful for seeing general trends. But it misses the quality of engagement—whether someone is actually thinking deeply or just scrolling. It can't capture offline studying, personal struggles, or the mental effort someone puts in. It's like seeing footprints but not knowing the journey.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and apply what I'm learning, not just completing tasks mechanically. It's when I find myself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, or when I get curious about going deeper. For me, engagement also means persisting even when things are difficult. It's an internal commitment that doesn't always show up in external metrics.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I sacrificed to stay in this course—the sleep I lost, the extra shifts I worked to afford materials, the family support I leaned on. It wouldn't show my handwritten notebooks full of rewritten concepts or the phone calls with classmates working through problems together. It definitely wouldn't show the resilience it took to keep going when I wanted to quit. My engagement was so much more than clicks and timestamps.
